# Transportation Impact Brief: Tom (PERSONA-006 — Fiscal Watchdog)

## Executive Summary

The reconfiguration was presented as a budget savings measure. At the higher-impact configurations, transportation-related fiscal exposure runs between **43% and 112% of the district's claimed savings** — meaning the restructuring could cost as much as it saves, or more. Critically, a portion of that cost has been shifted off the district's books and onto individual families, making the "savings" figure misleading without a full accounting. The board voted on this restructuring before anyone had counted the available bus drivers or modeled the routes.

---

## Key Finding

**Under Option A, total transportation fiscal exposure is $946,449–$1,679,845 per year.**

At the high end, that exceeds 100% of the claimed savings. Even at the low end, it consumes 43 cents of every dollar saved. This figure combines district-borne costs ($802,809–$876,325) and costs transferred to families ($143,640–$803,520). Only the district portion appears in the budget. The family portion does not.

---

## Configuration Comparison

| | Option A | Variant C | Option B |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SEA driver shortfall** | 10–13 drivers short | 9–12 drivers short | 4–7 drivers short |
| **Route expansion cost (district)** | $748,472 | $623,726 | $0 |
| **McKinney-Vento exposure (district)** | $54,337–$127,853/yr | $46,347–$111,871/yr | $30,365–$79,908/yr |
| **Care gap cost (families, not district)** | $143,640–$803,520/yr | $126,540–$714,240/yr | $0 |
| **Total fiscal exposure** | $946,449–$1,679,845/yr | $796,613–$1,449,837/yr | $30,365–$79,908/yr |
| **As % of claimed savings** | 43%–112% | 36%–97% | 1.4%–5.3% |
| **Families split across buildings** | 139–169 | 123–150 | 0 |

Option B is the only configuration where fiscal exposure is clearly bounded and demonstrably below claimed savings. It is also the only configuration with no driver shortfall risk at minimum — though even Option B requires 4–7 drivers the district does not currently have.

---

## What's Missing

The district has not produced analysis on four items that directly affect whether the claimed savings are real:

1. **No net savings disclosure.** The administration stated it does not "anticipate [route cost changes] being a significant cost" (TC-005, March 2 forum) without providing a supporting calculation. As of the board vote, no line-item reconciliation of gross savings versus transportation costs had been published.

2. **No per-household tax impact.** The budget presentation does not translate the fiscal exposure range into mil rate or per-assessed-value terms. Tom cannot know whether he is looking at a $40 or a $140 annual property tax consequence without this conversion.

3. **No driver sourcing plan.** The Director of Operations confirmed 20 bus drivers on March 30, 2026 — after the board voted. Every configuration requires more. Option A needs 30. No plan has been presented for how the district recruits 4–13 additional drivers in a tight labor market, or what that will cost.

4. **No before/after care capacity analysis prior to the vote.** After-care is full at 4 of 5 schools. The $143,640–$803,520 annual family cost burden under Option A is not a district line item — it never appeared in a budget document. The district's own survey showed 57% of parents worried about before/after care disruption (TC-011), yet no cost model was prepared.

---

## Limitations

This analysis is built on enrollment ranges, not confirmed route assignments. The McKinney-Vento exposure figures ($54K–$128K under Option A) use 10% MV-eligibility applied to displaced students — a reasonable estimate, but the actual count depends on which specific students are reassigned and whether their rights are triggered. The care gap cost range ($144K–$804K) is wide because it depends on private care rates and actual family demand, neither of which the district has surveyed. The route expansion cost ($748,472 for Option A) is based on available driver wage data and route modeling; it is the single figure in this analysis most sensitive to negotiated SEA contract terms.

South Portland's per-pupil transportation costs rose **50.2% from FY23 to FY25** ($709 → $1,065), compared to a statewide average increase of 10.5%. The structural cause of that divergence has not been explained in any budget document reviewed.

---

## Invitation to Improve

The district can sharpen every figure in this brief by releasing:

- The full transportation route model the consultant is building, including driver counts, tier assignments, and cost-per-route
- A reconciled net savings table that separates district-borne costs from costs transferred to families
- The current SEA contract terms governing driver hours, overtime triggers, and hiring authority
- Before/after care enrollment capacity data by school, by grade band
- The mil rate conversion for the fiscal exposure range, expressed per $100,000 of assessed value

This analysis does not oppose or support any configuration. It documents what has not been calculated — and what taxpayers would need to evaluate whether the promised savings are real.
